---
title: "Total Variation Regularization"
subtitle: "Chapter 8"
---

[Slides](../slides/week-08-total-variation.html) | [Notebook](../notebooks/week08_total_variation.ipynb) | [Open in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week08_total_variation.ipynb)

## Learning Goals

By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

- define Total Variation;
- compare TV with quadratic smoothness;
- explain why TV preserves edges better;
- identify staircasing artifacts;
- use TV as a modeling prior for image denoising.

## Edges Are Not Noise

Quadratic smoothing uses

$$
E(u)
=
\frac12\|u-y\|_2^2
+
\frac{\lambda}{2}\|\nabla u\|_2^2.
$$

This removes noise, but it also penalizes true edges. Edges and noise both create local changes, so a model must distinguish between unwanted oscillation and meaningful discontinuity.

Total Variation changes the regularizer.

## Continuous Total Variation

For a grayscale image $u:\Omega\to\mathbb{R}$,

$$
\operatorname{TV}(u)
=
\int_\Omega |\nabla u(x)|\,dx.
$$

TV measures the total amount of change in the image.

For a pixel grid, the isotropic discrete TV is

$$
\operatorname{TV}(u)
=
\sum_i
\sqrt{(D_xu_i)^2+(D_yu_i)^2}.
$$

Here $D_x$ and $D_y$ are finite differences in horizontal and vertical directions.

## TV Denoising

The classical Rudin-Osher-Fatemi model is

$$
u_\lambda
=
\operatorname*{argmin}_u
\frac12\|u-y\|_2^2
+
\lambda\,\operatorname{TV}(u).
$$

The data fidelity term keeps the reconstruction close to the noisy data. The TV term controls the total amount of change.

This regularizer says: among images that fit the data, prefer images with small total variation.

## l2 Versus TV Penalties

For a one-dimensional gradient value $g$, compare:

$$
\text{quadratic penalty}=\frac12 g^2,
\qquad
\text{TV penalty}=|g|.
$$

::: {.figure}
![](../assets/figures/week08-penalties.png){fig-alt="Quadratic and TV penalties for a gradient value and their derivative behavior"}

Large gradients are much more expensive under a quadratic penalty than under TV.
:::

For example, if $g=4$, then $\frac12g^2=8$, while $|g|=4$. The difference becomes larger as $|g|$ grows.

This is the basic reason TV preserves edges better: it penalizes the existence of change, but it is less aggressive toward large jumps than a squared-gradient model.

## Nonsmoothness

The function $|g|$ is not differentiable at $g=0$. This is not a minor technicality. It changes the optimization problem.

Quadratic smoothing leads to smooth linear equations. TV denoising is convex but nonsmooth. That makes it powerful for edge preservation and more delicate to solve.

Optimization methods for nonsmooth convex problems appear in the next chapter.

## One-Dimensional Edge Preservation

TV is easiest to understand on a step signal.

::: {.figure}
![](../assets/figures/week08-step-l2-tv.png){fig-alt="Noisy step signal denoised by quadratic smoothing and TV"}

Quadratic smoothing rounds jumps, while TV keeps flatter regions and sharper transitions.
:::

TV is well matched to piecewise-constant structure. It counts the amount of change, not how spread out the change is. A sharp jump can be cheaper than a long gradual transition with many small changes.

This is useful when the image contains objects separated by edges.

## Image Denoising

The contrast between quadratic smoothing and TV also appears on images.

::: {.figure}
![](../assets/figures/week08-image-l2-tv.png){fig-alt="Original image, noisy image, l2 smoothed image, and TV denoised image"}

TV removes noise while preserving stronger edge sets better than quadratic smoothing.
:::

Gradient maps make the behavior easier to see.

::: {.figure}
![](../assets/figures/week08-gradient-maps.png){fig-alt="Gradient magnitude maps of original, noisy, l2 smoothed, and TV denoised images"}

Noise creates many small gradients. TV suppresses weak fluctuations while retaining stronger edges.
:::

## Choosing The TV Weight

In

$$
\frac12\|u-y\|_2^2+\lambda\operatorname{TV}(u),
$$

larger $\lambda$ means:

- stronger denoising;
- fewer small variations;
- greater risk of losing detail.

::: {.figure}
![](../assets/figures/week08-parameter-effects.png){fig-alt="TV denoising with several regularization weights"}

The best value of $\lambda$ depends on the task.
:::

For a photograph, a visually pleasing balance may be appropriate. For a diagnostic image, oversmoothing a small structure may be unacceptable. Parameter choice is therefore both mathematical and domain dependent.

## Staircasing

TV favors sparse gradients. That means many locations with zero gradient and a smaller number of jumps.

::: {.figure}
![](../assets/figures/week08-staircasing.png){fig-alt="Smooth noisy signal denoised by l2 smoothing and TV, showing TV staircasing"}

TV can turn smooth variation into piecewise-flat plateaus.
:::

This staircasing effect is the signature artifact of TV. TV preserves edges, but it can create artificial plateaus in regions that should vary smoothly.

## Strengths And Weaknesses

| Property | l2 smoothing | TV |
|---|---|---|
| Noise removal | yes | yes |
| Edge preservation | weak | strong |
| Optimization | smooth linear problem | nonsmooth convex problem |
| Typical artifact | blur | staircasing |
| Best for | smooth images | piecewise-smooth or piecewise-constant images |

TV may struggle with texture, fine repeated patterns, gradual shading, curved smooth surfaces, and strongly nonlocal structure.

This is not a failure of mathematics. It is a reminder that every regularizer is a modeling choice.

## Course Checkpoint

At this point in the course, students should be able to connect:

- image arrays and forward models;
- convolution, blur, and Fourier interpretation;
- noise models and likelihoods;
- ill-posedness and singular values;
- Tikhonov, variational energies, and TV.

TV is the bridge from smooth quadratic models to nonsmooth optimization and sparse reconstruction.

## Computation

The Week 8 notebook lets you change the TV weight, compare TV and l2 smoothing, inspect gradient maps, and find staircasing examples.

Run:

```bash
python3 examples/week08_total_variation.py
```

or open the notebook in Colab from the link at the top of this chapter.

## Exercises

1. Write the continuous definition of $\operatorname{TV}(u)$.
2. Write the discrete isotropic TV formula using $D_xu_i$ and $D_yu_i$.
3. Compare the quadratic and TV costs for gradient values $g=1$ and $g=4$.
4. Explain why TV preserves edges better than quadratic smoothing.
5. Describe staircasing and give one situation where it would be undesirable.

## Takeaways

- Total Variation uses $\operatorname{TV}(u)=\int_\Omega |\nabla u|\,dx$.
- TV favors images with small total change.
- Compared with l2 smoothing, TV preserves stronger edges.
- TV is convex but nonsmooth.
- TV can create staircasing artifacts, so it is not always the right prior.
